items.q: What is the 'dark figure' of crime?
items.correct: The amount of crime that is unreported or unrecorded.
items.why: Students take 'dark' literally. It's a methodological concept - official statistics undercount crime because much goes unreported (victims fearful) or unrecorded (police discretion).

items.q: What is cultural capital according to Bourdieu?
items.correct: Cultural knowledge, tastes, and skills that confer advantage in education and society.
items.misconception: The amount of money parents spend on culture.
items.why: Students think 'capital' means money. Bourdieu used it metaphorically - cultural capital is non-economic (knowing how to 'play the game' in middle-class institutions like schools).

items.q: What does the chivalry thesis claim?
items.correct: Women are treated more leniently by the criminal justice system.
items.random_wrong: Men are oppressed by chivalrous laws.
items.why: Students think the thesis denies female criminality. It actually argues women's offences are UNDER-RECORDED because police/courts treat them paternalistically - it's about treatment, not behaviour.
It's Law. “Bus rule” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Norm. “Tide” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: UK age of criminal responsibility is, in years?
items.why: The real figure is 10. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
